coredata.dc:title: Inhibition of oxidative stress in vivo through enzyme-like activity of carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Dong, Chen
Wang, Shuna
Ma, Menghui
Wei, Peifa
Chen, Yan
Wu, Aiguo
Zha, Zhengbao
Bi, Hong
coredata.dc:description: Oxidative stress is a huge challenge to human health, and effective agents for resisting this adverse effect are remains limited recently. Here, an excellent enzyme-like activity of carbon dots (e-CDs) via a facile solvothermal method is reported. In addition, e-CDs exert protective effects in HUVEC cell model by decreasing apoptosis. e-CDs also efficiently diminish ROS and apoptosis damage in mice treated with D-galactose. Furthermore, e-CDs-pretreated mice shows decrease apoptotic proteins in the brain and liver, confirming that e-CDs ameliorate D-galactose induced aging. Compared with general nanozymes, biocompatible e-CDs demonstrate their high potential for anti-aging by the inhibition of oxidative stress.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Oxidative stress
Enzyme-like
Reactive oxygen species
Anti-aging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Free radicals scavenging activities of e-CDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundations of China (Nos. 51772001, 31971292, and 51873225), National Key R&D Program of China (Nos. 2018YFC0910601 and 2019YFA0405603), and the University Synergy Innovation Program of Anhui Province (Nos. GXXT-2019-045 and GXXT-2020-063). We also acknowledge support of the Key Laboratory of Environment Friendly Polymer Materials of Anhui Province and Anhui Key Laboratory of Modern Biomanufacturing, Hefei, China.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and the corresponding particle size distribution histogram of e-CDs. (b) FT-IR and (c) XPS spectra of the e-CDs. (d) HR XPS C1s and (e) O1s spectra of e-CDs and fitting results. (f) UV-Vis absorption and PL spectra of e-CDs (200 μg mL−1) dispersed in methanol (inset: photographs of e-CDs solution under visible daylight (Vis) and an UV light (UV)).

(a) Determination of total antioxidant capacity of e-CDs (Trolox as a reference). (b) The change curve of total antioxidant capacity with various concentration of e-CDs. The effect was evaluated spectrophotometrically at λ = 593 nm. (c) ●OH, (d) O2 ●−, and (e) ABTS+• removal test. Data shown are mean values and standard deviations from three independent experiments (n = 3). (f) EPR spectra of DMPO/HO• adducts were collected after incubation for 1 min with different samples. The concentrations of Fe2+ and H2O2 are both 20 μmol L−1.

(a) MTT results of HUVEC and HepG2 cells co-incubated with various concentrations (0–300 μg mL−1) of e-CDs for 24 h, respectively. (b) LDH release from the HUVEC and HepG2 cells treated with e-CDs at various concentrations (0–300 μg mL−1), respectively. (c) The protective effect of e-CDs to HUVEC cells from oxidative stress (HUVEC cells were incubated with 300 μmol L−1 H2O2 for 1 h). (d) Fluorescence images of HUVEC cells with ROS staining by DCFH-DA probe. (e) Fluorescence images of live and dead HUVEC cells stained with Calcein-AM and PI dyes, respectively. (f) The ROS level was determined by flow cytometry with DCFH-DA dye. Effect of e-CDs on (g) MDA, (h) GSH and (i) SOD in HUVEC cells. Data shown are mean values and standard deviations from three independent experiments (n = 3).

(a) Schematic diagram of oxidative damage model in mice. Effects of e-CDs on (b) MDA, (c) GSH-Px, (d) CAT and (e) SOD level in serum and liver tissues of D-galactose induced mice (VC as a reference). Effect of e-CDs on expression of Bax and Bcl-2 proteins in D-galactose induced mice (f) hippocampus and (g) liver. Magnification: × 200. Effect of e-CDs on the content of (h) elastic fibers and (i) collagen fibers in D-galactose induced mice skin tissue. Data shown are mean values and standard deviations from three independent experiments (n = 3).
Schematic illustration of e-CDs with various free radicals scavenging activities in vitro and in vivo.

head.ce:title: Inhibition of oxidative stress in vivo through enzyme-like activity of carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Key Laboratory of Environment Friendly Polymer Materials of Anhui Province, Anhui Key Laboratory of Modern Biomanufacturing, Anhui University, Hefei 230601, China

Zhejiang Engineering Research Center for Biomedical Materials
Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bCixi Institute of Biomedical Engineering, International Cooperation Base of Biomedical Materials Technology and Application, CAS Key Laboratory of Magnetic Materials and Devices, Zhejiang Engineering Research Center for Biomedical Materials, Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Ningbo 315201, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Energy Science and Technology, Guangdong Laboratory, Huizhou 516003, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food and Biological Engineering, Hefei University of Technology, Hefei 230009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food and Biological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: dSchool of Food and Biological Engineering, Hefei University of Technology, Hefei 230009, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Oxidative stress is a huge challenge to human health, and effective agents for resisting this adverse effect are remains limited recently. Here, an excellent enzyme-like activity of carbon dots (e-CDs) via a facile solvothermal method is reported. In addition, e-CDs exert protective effects in HUVEC cell model by decreasing apoptosis. e-CDs also efficiently diminish ROS and apoptosis damage in mice treated with D-galactose. Furthermore, e-CDs-pretreated mice shows decrease apoptotic proteins in the brain and liver, confirming that e-CDs ameliorate D-galactose induced aging. Compared with general nanozymes, biocompatible e-CDs demonstrate their high potential for anti-aging by the inhibition of oxidative stress.

ce:section.ce:para:
Aging has severe influences on human health and has physiological features, such as cardiovascular function decline, organ aging, and learning ability decline [1]. Although aging is inevitable, it is feasible to delay aging. Current research believes that aging is the result of comprehensive factors such as stem cell decline, DNA degradation, dietary spiritual factors, and active aging genes [2]. Among them, the view that the accumulation of free radicals causes DNA damage and leads to aging has attracted widespread attention [3]. Reactive oxygen species (ROS), including hydroxyl radicals (●OH), superoxide anion radicals (O2 ●
− ), and singlet oxygen (1O2), play a significant role in various physiological processes [4]. However, excessive ROS in the body can cause serious pathological consequences such as aging and cancers by disrupting cellular components and normal functions [5]. Thus, scavenging excessive ROS is essential for maintaining normal body function.

Carbon dots (CDs) are widely used in nanomedicine due to their ultra-small size, excellent tunable optical properties, high water-solubility, and good biocompatibility [6–9]. Recently, multiple reports have been demonstrated that CDs exhibit free radical generating or scavenging ability based on their intrinsic enzyme-like activities [10–12]. CDs or other nanomaterials with enzyme-like properties have attracted considerable attention because of their high-stability and lower cost than natural enzymes [13–17]. Until now, there are two main strategies to achieve or improve the ROS scavenging activity of CDs as follows. Oxygen-containing functional groups (e.g., O=C−OH) can promote the adsorption of ROS and are conducive to the formation of adducts and electron transfer between ROS and carbon-based nanomaterials [18,19]. Li et al. prepared CDs with effectively free radicals scavenging ability by microwave-assisted method [20]. The CDs with abundant −OH active groups on their surface could scavenge free radicals by transferring free electrons from free radicals to the C−C backbone in the core. Alternatively, heteroatom/metal doping has been an effective strategy to promote their radical clearance ability because the dopants can facilitate electron transfer [21,22]. In this regard, Rosenkrans et al. demonstrated that Se doped CDs have high-performance ROS scavenging ability, which inhibit cell damage from oxidative stress to improved cell viability [23]. Nevertheless, research on CDs-based ROS scavenger is still in its infancy, but the prospects are promising.

Here, a new type of CDs with enzyme-like activity (e-CDs) is synthesized via one-pot solvothermal method from carmine cochineal (Scheme 1). Various oxygenated groups including abundant carboxyl and carbonyl groups in the precursor endowed e-CDs with ultrahigh enzyme-like activity. Additionally, previous work has been confirmed that the unique anthraquinone structure have predominant antioxidation effect [24]. Significantly, the as-synthesized e-CDs exhibit excellent scavenging ability against various negative and cationic free radicals both in vitro. By using D-galactose induced aging mice as a typical model, these e-CDs with excellent anti-aging capacity. We expected that this study could yield valuable insight for further work into the development of various novel CDs with high-performance enzyme-like activity via facile and efficient synthesis strategies, as well as provides new thinking for the application of nanomaterials in anti-aging drugs.

Carmine cochineal was obtained from Zhejiang Yi Nuo Biotechnology Co., Ltd. FeCl3•6H2O, N, N-Dimethylformamide, pyrogallol, 6-hydroxy-2,5,7,8-tetramethylchroman-2-carboxylic acid, 2,2′-Azinobis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid ammonium salt), 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide, tris (hydroxymethyl) aminomethane hydrochloride, and 2,4,6-tris(2-pyridyl)-s-triazine were purchased from Aladdin Chemicals Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). NaCl, FeSO4•6H2O, K2S2O8, H2O2 (30 wt.%), salicylic acid, acetic acid, sodium acetate, ascorbic acid, ethanol, and methanol were purchased by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Dimethyl sulfoxide was obtained from Beijing Solarbio Science and Technology Co., Ltd. Dulbecco's modified Eagle medium, penicillin/streptomycin, and fetal bovine serum were purchased from Hyclone. 3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2, 5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Chemicals. The lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) assay kit was purchased from Nanjing Jian Chen BioChem Co. HUVEC cells was obtained from ZQXZBIO, Shanghai, China. All chemicals were used as received without further purification unless otherwise specified. All water used during the experiment was ultrapure water from the microporous system.

The morphology of the sample was evaluated using a JEM-2100 TEM. FT-IR spectra were collected using a NEXUS-870 spectrometer. XPS was conducted with a commercial spectrophotometer (ESCALAB 250). The UV-Vis spectra were obtained with a Shanghai Meipuda Spectrophotometer (UV-1800PC). PL measurements were performed on a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. EPR spectra were obtained with a Bruker EMX nano X-band Bench-top CW EPR spectrometer at 9.4 GHz X band and measured at 25 °C, with magnetic field scanning in the range from 10 to 600 mT.

The e-CDs were synthesized through a facile one-pot solvothermal method. Briefly, carmine cochineal (0.5 g, the molecular structure as shown in Fig. S1) was first dissolved in N, N-Dimethylformamide, and the solution was transferred into poly (tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclaves. After heating at 160 °C in an oven for 8 h, the solution was then allowed to naturally cool to room temperature naturally. Further purification was conducted by dialysis using a dialysis membrane (MWCO 1000) for 96 h, followed by freeze-drying to obtain a red powdered product.

The free radicals scavenging capacity of e-CDs was determined by the reduction of Fe3+ to Fe2+, according to the method described by Benzie and Strain [25]. Three typical free radicals (●OH, O2 ●−, ABTS+ •) were employed here to evaluate the scavenging activities of e-CDs [26].

Both HUVEC and HepG2 cells were maintained in DMEM containing 1% penicillin/streptomycin and 10% FBS in a 95% humidified atmosphere incubator (Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA) with 5% CO2 at 37 °C. Red blood cells (RBCs) were isolated from the whole mouse blood by centrifugation at 2000 rpm for 5 min, and resuspended in 0.9% NaCl solution. Typical MTT, LDH and hemolysis assays were systemically evaluated according to standard protocols reports [27].

ROS elimination of e-CDs was detected by DCFH-DA probe. Fluorescence of the HUVEC cells was imaged by a fluorescence microscope. Protective effect against H2O2-induced oxidative damage of e-CDs was evaluated by Calcein-AM and PI dyes. Intracellular ROS levels were measured by flow cytometry from cells cultured in serum-free medium. The MDA, GSH, and SOD levels were measured by commercial assay kit.

All animal handlings were under the guidance and approval of the Laboratory Animal Center of Anhui Medical University with an animal experiment license number of SYXK (Wan) 2020-001. After 30 days, mice were sacrificed, their organs were taken out and soaked in 4% paraformaldehyde for paraffin section. Samples were prepared into paraffin section (5 μm transversely or 8 μm coronally) for histological evaluation. The MDA, SOD, CAT, and GSH-Px in serum and tissues of each group were measured by commercial assay kit. Meanwhile, the level of tissue protein in each group was determined by the total protein (TP) assay kit. Immunohistochemical analysis of brain and liver tissue was performed. In addition, Weigert's resorcinol magenta method was used to stain the elastic fibers of mice skin. The modified method of Mallory-Heidenhain was used for staining collagen fibers.

As shown in Fig. 1 a, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the as-prepared e-CDs are uniform in shape with an average diameter of 4.80 nm. An average lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which is corresponding to the (100) facet of graphite [28], can be clearly observed in the high-resolution TEM image of e-CDs (Fig. S2). The chemical structure of the e-CDs was determined by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1b), the absorption band at 3389 cm −
1 attributed to the stretching vibrations of −OH [29]. Two strong absorption peaks located at 1588 and 1347 cm −
1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of C=O and C−O bonds, respectively [30]. The XPS further confirm the above-mentioned FT-IR results. As shown in Fig. 1c, the e-CDs mainly consist of C (285.3 eV, at.% = 68.1) and O (530.2 eV, at.% = 31.9) elements. In Fig. 1d, the high-resolution C 1s spectrum reveals three peaks at 284.6, 286.7, and 288.9 eV assigned to C−C/C=C, C−O, and C=O, respectively [31]. The HR XPS O 1s spectrum has two peaks, which are attributed to C=O (531.8 eV) and C−O (535.7 eV) bonds, respectively (Fig. 1e) [32]. Subsequently, optical properties of e-CDs were investigated. As shown in Fig. 1f, the absorption peaks at 210 and 283 nm are ascribed to π − π ⁎ and n − π ⁎, respectively [33]. The corresponding photoluminescence (PL) spectrum shows two emissions (pink fluorescence) at 553 and 585 nm. Besides, e-CDs presented significant excitation-independent under different excitation wavelengths, suggested that the emission was originated from individual emitting state (Fig. S3) [34–36]. The as-synthesized e-CDs can be well dissolved in solvents with high polarity (e.g., water and dimethyl sulfoxide) because of containing more groups such as −OH and O=C−OH on the surface (Fig. S4) [37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Free radicals scavenging activities of e-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The capacity of e-CDs to scavenge various free radicals was evaluated. The total antioxidant capacity of e-CDs was investigated through ferric reducing ability of plasma (FRAP) assay. Fig. 2 a and b shows the Trolox molar equivalence in the reduction of ferric iron with various concentrations of e-CDs, compared to the absorbance obtained for the calibration curve (Fig. S5). ●OH radicals scavenging properties of e-CDs and ascorbic acid (as a reference) were determined by collecting absorbance at 510 nm. As seen in Fig. S6, the intensity of absorbance at 510 nm shows a decrease with the increase in the concentration of e-CDs, and the color of the solution changes from deep purple to lavender accordingly. Growth in ●OH radical scavenging capacity is observed from 23 to 69% with the increase in concentrations (Fig. 2c). Similarly, Fig. 2d shows the highest O2 ●
− scavenging activity of e-CDs is 68% at a concentration of 300 μg mL−1 (more details are shown in Fig. S7). Moreover, the ABTS radical scavenging (ABTS+•) effects of e-CDs are presented in Figs. 2e and S8. Ultimately, the maximum ratio of the radical scavenging activity of e-CDs towards ABTS+• is calculated to be about 86%. Afterwards, the free radical scavenging activity of e-CDs was assessed with EPR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 2f, a typical four-split pattern EPR spectrum with relative intensities of 1:2:2:1 was observed, indicating the formation of DMPO/●OH radical adducts. Apparently, the radical's EPR signal intensity was dramatically reduced when e-CDs are introduced, which demonstrate that e-CDs possess excellent ●OH scavenging capacity. Combined with previous reports [38–40], the CD are considered as an excellent electron donor and electron acceptor and thus play an important role in the process of ROS scavenging by electron transfer. Comprehensive survey of previously reported CDs with ROS scavenging studies are summarized in Table S1. It can be inferred that most of the CDs own scavenging ability for anion free radicals, while the CDs with scavenging ability for multiple free radicals are still in the exploratory stage.

To assess the potential applications of as-prepared e-CDs in biological systems, cytotoxicity and hemolysis studies were performed to investigate their biocompatibility in vitro. The cell viability of HUVEC and HepG2 cells after 24 h incubation with e-CDs was investigated using the MTT assay, respectively. The results show that more than 80% cells still remain even with the concentration of e-CDs reaching 300 μg mL −
1, which agrees well with previous investigations (Fig. 3 a) [41]. Additionally, the amount of lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) released into the culture medium by e-CDs cross the cell membrane was detected via LDH kit to determine the toxicity to cells [42]. As shown in Fig. 3b, the release of LDH in both types of cells exhibit a concentration-dependent effect, and the maximum release was less than 300 U L −
1. These results further prove that e-CDs induce little damage to cell membrane, which is consistent with the results of MTT assay. Besides, hemolysis test was conducted to evaluate the blood biocompatibility of e-CDs. Negligible hemolytic activity emerged for blood cells incubated with e-CDs, and the highest hemolysis ratio is 6.8%, suggesting the suitability of e-CDs for blood applications (Fig. S9). The above results demonstrated high potential of e-CDs for bio-medical applications.

To evaluate whether e-CDs were able to protect HUVEC cells, oxidative stress was induced with H2O2 (Fig. 3c). HUVEC cells were cultured with 300 μmol L−1 H2O2, which decreased cell viability by approximately 50%. However, the cell viability gradient increase after adding e-CDs to the culture medium. When the concentration of e-CDs was increase to 300 μg mL−1, no significant difference in cell viability compared to the control could be observed, indicating that there is almost no adverse ROS production during incubation. The protective effects of e-CDs are attributed to decreased intracellular ROS production, as shown in Fig. 3d. ROS levels were assessed during incubation using 2’, 7’-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) as ROS probe. After introducing H2O2 to the cell culture medium, strong green fluorescence could be significantly observed while no fluorescence emerges in the control and e-CDs groups. Interestingly, upon mixing H2O2 and e-CDs (200 μg mL−1), only weak green fluorescence could be observed, and no fluorescence emerges when the concentration of e-CDs is 300 μg mL−1. To further confirm the above findings, the protective effect of e-CDs on H2O2-induced oxidative stress damage was evaluated by staining with calcein-AM and propidium iodide (PI) dyes. As shown in Fig. 3e, H2O2 could induce a large amount of cell death after incubation for 4 h, and yet no obvious cell death occurs upon addition of e-CDs. These results suggest that e-CDs exhibit excellent ability to scavenge ROS and show a significant protective effect on cells. Furthermore, flow cytometry was employed for quantitative analysis of H2O2-induced ROS generation in HUVEC cells with DCFH-DA (Fig. 3f) [43]. HUVEC cells in the absence of e-CDs were used as control that exhibit fluorescence in the range between 102 and 104. The results show that HUVEC cells cultured with H2O2 alone emit the strongest fluorescence signal. However, fluorescence intensity of HUVEC cells decrease gradually after mixed culture of H2O2 and different concentrations of e-CDs.

Next, the effects of e-CDs on malondialdehyde (MDA), glutathione (GSH), and superoxide dismutase (SOD) levels in HUVEC cells was evaluated. MDA is the main product of lipid peroxidation in the body, and can be used as an important indicator to assess the degree of peroxidation damage. As shown in Fig. 3g, the content of MDA in the cells significantly increase after H2O2 treatment, while the level of MDA in the cells gradually reduces after the introduction of e-CDs. The results showed that e-CD effectively prevent the oxidative damage caused by H2O2 to cells. The level of GSH in cells can be used as another important indicator to measure the degree of cellular oxidative stress. As can be seen from Fig. 3h, the GSH content of HUVEC cells drop sharply by about 30% under the stimulation of H2O2, while GSH content increases by an obvious gradient after the addition of e-CDs. In addition, SOD is the representative of cell protective enzymes, which can effectively prevent high intracellular ROS level through synergistic action. As shown in Fig. 3i, e-CDs effectively inhibit the decrease of SOD activity induced by H2O2. All the above results suggest that the e-CD behave as an excellent ROS scavenger and protect the cells from oxidative stress damage.

Inspired by the appreciable inhibit oxidative stress effect in vitro, anti-aging experiment was conducted in vivo deploying D-galactose induced oxidative damage models (Fig. 4
a). Thirty-six mice were randomly divided into six groups: Control, VC (injection of 300 mg kg−1 D-galactose, oral of 300 mg kg−1 VC), D-galactose (injection of 300 mg kg−1 D-galactose), e-CDs-1 (injection of 300 mg kg−1 D-galactose, oral of 100 mg kg−1 e-CDs), e-CDs-2 (injection of 300 mg kg−1 D-galactose, oral of 200 mg kg−1 e-CDs) and e-CDs-3 (injection of 300 mg kg−1 D-galactose, oral of 300 mg kg−1 e-CDs). After 30 days of consecutive treatment, the blood and tissues from different groups were collected. The level of MDA, glutathione peroxidase (GSH-Px), catalase (CAT) and SOD in serum and liver of D-galactose induced oxidative damage models were used to evaluate the inhibit oxidative stress effect in vivo. Compared with the control, MDA levels in serum and liver of D-galactose induced oxidative damage models significantly increase (Fig. 4b). Conversely, upon the oral administration of e-CDs or VC after D-galactose treatment, the level of MDA significantly decrease, much closer to the level of the control. GSH-Px is a kind of biological enzyme that can degrade peroxide, which is widely distributed in the body. It converts the reduced GSH into oxidized glutathione (GSSG), which converts and reduces the peroxide to no relatively harmless hydroxy compounds in the cell membrane structure, thereby preventing the body from being damaged by peroxides. As shown in Fig. 4c, e-CDs effectively increase the level of GSH-Px to prevent oxidative damage caused by D-galactose in mice. CAT is an enzyme that efficiently catalyzes the decomposition of H2O2 and plays an important role in the process of scavenging ROS. The results show that with increasing e-CDs concentration, the levels of CAT in serum and liver tissue of mice gradually increase, indicating that oxidative damage is effectively alleviated (Fig. 4d). Similarly, e-CDs can alleviate D-galactose induced oxidative damage in mice by increasing SOD levels as shown in Fig. 4e. To further explore the biocompatibility in vivo, major organs, including heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney, were collected from different groups after treatment on day 30. After pathological examination via H&E staining, there is no detectable signs of organ damage were observed for the e-CDs treatment group, indicating no damage to normal organs in 30 days (Fig. S10).

Generally, the expression of Bax and Bcl-2 proteins is the key factor in cell apoptosis. Immunohistochemical staining show that the expression level of Bax protein in hippocampus of aging mice induced by D-galactose is significantly higher than the control, while the expression level of Bcl-2 protein significantly decreases (Fig. 4f). Notably, the expression of Bax protein in the hippocampus of aging mice treated with e-CDs significantly decreases, and the expression of Bcl-2 relatively increases. These results indicate that D-galactose could accelerate the aging of mice, while e-CDs could reduce the apoptotic proteins, increase the formation of anti-apoptotic proteins, and alleviate the apoptosis of cells in the brain tissue of mice, thus alleviating the aging of the body. The results in Fig. 4g are consistent with the above-mentioned findings; the e-CDs also have a certain alleviating effect on the anti-apoptosis of liver tissue of mice induced by D-galactose.

In addition, the pathological morphology of the skin tissue of the mice was observed and analyzed. As shown in Fig. 4h, the elastic fibers in the skin tissue staining sections of the e-CDs-2 group are better than D-galactose induced oxidative damage models. The elastic fibers are clearly observed to be neatly arranged and slender, and some are interwoven or reticulated. In comparison, collagen fibers in the skin tissue of the D-galactose induced oxidative damage models are significantly reduced and arranged irregularly (Fig. 4i) [44]. Some areas are broken and sparse, which is a typical expression of aging characteristics. After e-CDs treatment, the collagen fibers in the skin tissues of mice improve to a certain extent. Most of the collagen fibers are wavy and orderly arranged, with improved density. These results suggest that the aging damage of mice skin tissue induced by D-galactose could be alleviated to a certain extent under e-CDs treatment, and the normal physiological structure and morphology could be maintained.

In summary, we have developed a new type of e-CDs exhibited high-performance enzyme-like activity. In vitro, the e-CDs exhibited excellent scavenging ability against various free radicals. In vivo, the e-CDs efficiently diminished D-galactose induced ROS and apoptosis damage. Meanwhile, the aging mice treated with the e-CDs significantly via effective scavenging of ROS, and suppression of oxidative stress injury. The present study indicates that e-CDs inhibit oxidative stress damage in vitro and in vivo through enzyme-like activity.

Chen Dong: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Shuna Wang: Resources, Validation. Menghui Ma: Visualization, Investigation. Peifa Wei: Supervision. Yan Chen: Resources. Aiguo Wu: Supervision. Zhengbao Zha: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Hong Bi: Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundations of China (Nos. 51772001, 31971292, and 51873225), National Key R&D Program of China (Nos. 2018YFC0910601 and 2019YFA0405603), and the University Synergy Innovation Program of Anhui Province (Nos. GXXT-2019-045 and GXXT-2020-063). We also acknowledge support of the Key Laboratory of Environment Friendly Polymer Materials of Anhui Province and Anhui Key Laboratory of Modern Biomanufacturing, Hefei, China.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.apmt.2021.101178.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Lopez-Otin, M.A. Blasco, L. Partridge, M. Serrano, G. Kroemer, The hallmarks of aging, Cell153 (2013) 1194–1217.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Oxidants, oxidative stress and the biology of ageing

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Free radicals and antioxidants in normal physiological functions and human disease
